title: Reading and Use of English - Part 2
description: Complete the text with ONE word only.

content.text: From the earliest days of cinema, directors have sought ways to bring the impossible (0) life. Early pioneers like Georges Méliès used clever camera tricks to create illusions. (9) HOWEVER simple these techniques might seem to a modern audience, they were groundbreaking at the time. || (10) should a viewer watch these early films today, they would still find a certain charm in their hand-crafted nature. (11) IT is often argued that the transition to Computer Generated Imagery (CGI) has robbed cinema of its tactile quality. (12) AS far as some purists are concerned, practical effects are always superior to digital ones. (13) SUCH is the demand for realism in blockbuster movies that studios now spend hundreds of millions on visual effects. (14) NOT only do these tools allow for epic space battles, but they also enable subtle changes in lighting that the eye barely perceives. (15) ONLY provided that the story remains the focus, technology can be a powerful ally to the filmmaker. Ultimately, there is (16) NO point in using high-end effects if the narrative lacks emotional depth.

questions.explanation: Used to introduce a concessive adverbial clause. 'However + adjective + subject + verb' is a formal structure expressing contrast.
Inversion in a first conditional (Should a viewer watch...).
Dummy subject for the passive reporting structure 'It is argued that'.
Part of the fixed phrase 'As far as... are concerned'.
Inversion for emphasis to show degree: 'Such is the... that'.
Part of the negative inversion 'Not only... but also'.
Used to emphasize the condition 'Only provided that'.
Part of the fixed expression 'there is no point in'.
